# **Robotics in Agriculture: Challenges and Opportunities for Net-Zero**

![Femi Royal](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*olddNHuDLXr-YjhHynbsXg.jpeg)

--

Listen

Share

According to the World Bank, one of the most important and major sectors of the global economy is agriculture, and agricultural growth is one of the most important elements in reducing global poverty.

While in some nations, like India, agriculture can account for up to 18% of the national GDP, it only contributed 4% to the global economy in 2018 (World Bank, 2020). However, there are still pressing issues that the industry faces.

![]()

In 2050, the world’s population is expected to reach 9.1 billion, necessitating a 70% increase in food production, even though more than 820 million people are now deemed to be food insecure (FAO, 2020). Furthermore, the ageing of the farming population is a major worry because older generations are less likely to be replaced by younger ones when they retire. Other significant issues in agriculture include weed, irrigation, runoff, excessive insecticide use, insect and pathogen invasion, water scarcity, and climate change (Marinoudi et al., 2019).

One of the primary causes of climate change are agricultural practises, which alter how soil is used, deplete water supplies, and contaminate marine environments through excessive fertiliser and pesticide use (Springmann, 2018). Ecosystems have been made extinct by agricultural practises such as deforestation, the use of synthetic fertilisers, and animal pollution. With 70% of all water used globally going to agriculture, the sector is another big groundwater consumer. To boost crop output and protect the environment, the industry has created innovative concepts and approaches.

In the last 15 years, agriculture has started to go digital as well as migrate from being labor driven to mechanical and energy driven production systems. The usage of robotics and AI is considered a technique to automate tasks and enhance efficiency, but the industry has not yet advanced in comparison to current innovation (Talaviya et al., 2020).

**What are AI and robotics?**

Since John McCarthy first raised the subject in 1956, artificial intelligence (AI) has been a research area. Search algorithms, machine learning algorithms, and data analysis have all made major strides in the last 60 years. Machine learning (ML) is currently a sought-after subfields in AI due to the data quantity and really low cost of processing power. In order to create predictions, machine learning algorithms use statistical, algorithmic models to learn from a pool of training data (Jason, 2019).

The definition of AI has changed throughout time, and there is currently no agreed-upon definition. Definitions, however, can typically be divided into four categories. In the beginning, an AI system acts and thinks logically, much like a human would. The Turing Test was developed by Alan Turing in the 1950s to determine whether a machine is capable of thought. This test requires a computer to be capable of four tasks: natural language processing, information retrieval, analytics and machine learning, and computer vision. According to the third definition, AI is software that can endure in an erratic environment. Last but not least, AI can be applied to perceptual problems, automated coding and scheduling concerns, expert consulting systems, robotics, and intelligent database retrieval (Akshay et al., 2020).

Electronic, mechanical, and computer science engineering are all combined in the field of robotics. Robotics is the study of building, developing, manufacturing, and operating machines called robots that are intended to carry out difficult tasks (Jha et al., 2019). The first modern robots were introduced with the creation of robotic systems in the 1950s, despite the fact that such devices have been developed and refined throughout time (Nonami et al., 2014).

They were straightforward devices that carried out particular manufacturing tasks at a predefined location. Since then, robotics has expanded tremendously, finding use in a number of industries, including space exploration, medical, and military. The subject of robotics is continuously growing as technology advances.

**Agriculture robots and artificial intelligence history**

The use of AI and robotics in agriculture has recently been on the rise, and this development is not limited to that sector (Jha et al., 2019). Since 1983, when computers and other technologies first entered agriculture, numerous recommendations and theoretical ideas for reforming the sector have been put forth.

The 1920s saw the beginning of research into the incorporation of automatic driving direction, which is when the employment of robots in agriculture first emerged. (Yaghoubi et al., 2013). As a result, automatic farm vehicles were developed in the 1950s, despite the fact that the initial concepts were defective and needed a wire system to direct them. Machine vision guiding was made possible in the 1980s with the advancement of computers, and since then, the usage of robots in agriculture has risen.

Expert systems and software for agriculture that uses artificial intelligence first appeared in the 1980s. The Comax expert system, developed by Lemmon in 1986 to simulate the growth of cotton crops, is one famous example (Lemmon, 1986). Lemmon also created the Gossym programme, a Comax-compatible tool that is friendly to microcomputers and the first successful marriage of an expert system and computer model.

Researchers created the autonomous mobile robot AURORA in 1996. It may do specialist tasks requiring a lot of physical effort while moving or being guided automatically in greenhouses (Mandow et al., 2000). Herbicide, pesticide, and other industrial chemical exposure risks that greenhouse employees are generally exposed to have been decreased because to this technique.

The creation of “PRITHVI,” a fuzzy logic-based expert system created specifically for the soya bean crop in Rajasthan, India, is one of the more recent developments in AI and robotics in agriculture (Jha et al., 2019). This system analyses the entire system and directs farmers using the knowledge and experience of agricultural specialists. Crop management systems, yield forecasts, communications, and infestation identification systems have also advanced.

Overall, there is a long history of the use of AI and robots in agriculture, and it is certain that this tendency will only intensify with new technical developments.

**Applications of robotics and artificial intelligence in agriculture**

**Plant disease and pest detection and monitoring:** According to (Martinelli, 2015), crop diseases and insect infestations are a major worry for farmers because they can negatively affect crop yield and profitability. However, in-depth knowledge and experience are needed, and they might not always be available, for the early detection and precise diagnosis of these problems. Recent developments in machine learning (ML), a kind of AI, have made the identification of such diseases and pests early on relatively quick and simple to do. By training an artificial neural network on a large images meta-data depicting crop diseases and pests, the prediction model learning to enhance its predictive ability based on what it has learnt from this process (Rajan, 2016).

The use of a mobile application powered by a machine learning model is one technique to make use of this technology. The end-user takes a photo of the plant and uploads it for examination by the AI model. According on the plant’s visible indicators, the algorithm classifies pests and diseases. After a diagnosis, the app provides details on the diseases or pests, a description of the potential plant damage, and potential preventative measures and treatments (Rajan, 2016). These AI-powered smartphone applications could prompt faster diagnosis and warn farmers of potential pest and disease epidemics.

**Systems for precise robot weeding:** In agricultural farms, weeds coexist with food crops and can affect them in a number of ways, including by lowering crop quality, raising production costs, reducing crop output, and acting as a host for pests, parasites, and illnesses. The amount of freshwater available for crops to use is constrained since weeds use more freshwater than crops (NIOS, 2020).

Herbicides are sprayed in patches using the patch spraying technique, which saves money. Weedcer is a tool that analyses red-green-blue high-resolution photos to evaluate cereal and young weed leaves using a machine vision algorithm for weed monitoring. Patch spraying has been asserted to be more efficient than uniform herbicide treatment.

Weed monitoring techniques like patch spraying and site-specific weed control, which are based on maps and employ automatic image processing, are useful. However, variations in leaf forms, primarily caused by growth stages, have a negative impact on image processing. Weedcer can calculate the coverage of mayweed, broadleaf weeds overall, and cereal. Adigo’s GPS-guided field robot is used by Weedcer software to gather RGB photos and locations. SINTEF Information and Communication Technology initially developed the software, which Adigo AS later enhanced for real-time use. For an assessment of the relative weed cover, the RGB pictures are processed by the Weedcer algorithm (Berge et al., 2012).

![]()

(Bubar and Morrison, 1982) claim that because weed and crop root systems frequently entwine, the latter’s capacity to absorb water is impeded by the former. Although several methods have been developed over the years to make weeding more effective, it is still a difficult task. The most popular technique for locating and identifying weeds is computer vision. However, there are differences across studies in the types of crops or weeds, the complexity of the surrounding environment, and the detectors employed (Aktar et al., 2009).

This laborious procedure can be automated with robots driven by AI. Deep learning is used by the robots’ robotic arms, motors, and sensors to identify different types of weeds and tell them apart from the crops. This enables them to precisely locate and eliminate weeds without endangering the crops. Herbicide-resistant weeds (HRW) have caused losses of more than $500 million yearly. Historically, weed management focused on mechanical assistance, crop rotation, and the use of herbicides. Mechanical weeding is losing its effectiveness since it compacts the soil, making operations nearly difficult once the crop canopy has fully developed. On a broad scale, hand weeding is also impractical. Agbots, as opposed to heavy machinery, can manage HRW and weed between and within crops, whereas heavy machinery becomes ineffective after the canopy has fully developed. The robots can work in fields that are heavily populated and do not compact the soil or harm the plants as they drive over them. The robots have the ability to eradicate weeds before they reach maturity and disperse seeds (McAllister et al., 2019).

**Robotic Irrigation** — Since agriculture uses 70% of the world’s water resources, it is important to find workable solutions to ensure the efficient use of water resources for irrigation (World Bank, 2019). Population growth is accompanied by an increase in food demand. Traditional irrigation techniques based on soil moisture monitoring have been supplanted by automated water system scheduling techniques. Crop characteristics like growth rate, soil type, ground conditions, and infection, as well as meteorological variables like air humidity, air velocity, and sunlight exposure, are taken into account while building independent irrigation systems (Talaviya et al., 2020).

This autonomous system’s main goals are to decrease resource consumption and boost production and efficiency without a lot of human involvement. Cordless drip irrigators are used to install automatic irrigation systems in agricultural fields. To monitor the soil’s essential elements, such as potassium and phosphorus, and ascertain the soil fertility rate, fertility and soil pH detectors are implanted. This approach senses the groundwater table, soil conditions, nutritional appropriateness, and weather data to ensure the best possible use of water resources while maintaining soil nutrition and structure (Obaideen *et al.*, 2022).

**Automated Harvesting** — Harvesting is the process of gathering ripe product from agricultural grounds, which may be a time- and labor-intensive task during the planting season. This process is known as automated harvesting. In order to pick fruits without damaging them, harvesting robots are commonly equipped with prosthetic limbs and computer vision. This technology has been created to address the manpower crisis. Robots may have trouble harvesting fruit from hedges and herbaceous or arboreal plants, though. Contrary to popular belief, robots have been developed and tested for a variety of crops, including strawberries, tomatoes, asparagus, cucumber, lettuce, mushrooms, and oranges. Accurate fruit placement and recognition are essential for the development of efficient robotic harvest technology (Onishi, 2019).

Robotic harvesting can also aid in addressing workforce shortages, a major productivity obstacle that has been made particularly apparent in the UK as a result of Brexit and COVID-19-related labour movement concerns. Particularly the soft fruit industry has expressed interest in deploying autonomous robots, but producers’ willingness to adopt this technology will determine how quickly they are adopted. Over 2.5 million agricultural workers in the UK have lost their jobs in the last ten years, according to (David et al., 2023).

![]()

**Crop monitoring** — Improvements in sensing and imaging technologies have opened up new possibilities for farmers to increase crop productivity and reduce plant destruction. Surveying, data collecting, and analysis are increasingly being done with drones equipped with detectors and high lenses. These techniques are being tried and changed constantly. Unmanned aircraft that can be internally or externally controlled by a human control panel are referred to as aerial robots, or drones (Chen et al., 2019).

Farmers can identify agricultural illnesses, pests, nutritional deficiencies, excessive or insufficient water supplies, and other elements that might affect crop growth by employing drones. While satellite imagery has been used for aerial surveillance in agriculture for a long time, drones enable for more accurate and diverse data collection (Talaviya, 2020).

**Planting by robots:** Planting is the process of dispersing seeds over the soil’s surface to begin growing. Even while manual seeding or replanting is still employed in some circumstances, it is labour intensive. The seeds are typically dispersed evenly and at the right rate. However, a number of planting-automation robots have been developed and put to the test. These robots use a reverse cell plate to generate a seeding procedure as one of their tactics. The seed is placed by the robot in a plate that has spots for each cell’s matching numbers on it. This strategy is four times faster than the standard execution method with only a few seeding failures (Sakaue, 1996).

The only human input required for the autonomous farm robot version created by Naik and his research group is the measuring of the spacing between the plants and grain rows. To identify plant rows, the robot employs IR detectors (Sakaue, 1996). In greenhouses, considerable planting efforts are typically required because of the dense plant population and short life cycles of many planted species. Robotic automation of this procedure is essential (Ampatzidis, 2017).

**Impacts of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics on Agriculture**

**Social impact:**

Future human life and employment patterns will significantly change as a result of the creation of autonomous computers and robots using robotics and artificial intelligence. This shift is not an exception in the agricultural sector. As part of smart agriculture and the fourth industrial revolution, AI and robotics may result in job losses in the sector (Chen et al., 2019). But it also generates brand-new employment opportunities in industries like robot maintenance and repair.

Demographic shifts could happen as a result of the employment of agricultural robots. Employees may reside further away from the farm than was common in the past when new, more complex tasks are created (Rotz, 2019). Additionally, this change might make the income gap in rural areas worse (Howard, 2017). However, the use of robots might also result in a more equal distribution of men and women in the agricultural workforce since farm employment might become more accessible to women since physical strength would no longer be a major consideration. Given the underrepresentation of women in STEM professions, however, the need for STEM skills in agriculture might still be challenging for the female gender to participate in the modern farming systems (Smith, 2011).

**Economic Impact**

Robotics and artificial intelligence have the potential to greatly improve product quality and productivity in the agricultural sector, resulting in greater crop yields. From an economic perspective, this will have a big influence on the generally inefficient agri-food industry. The agri-food sector in the UK supported 3.7 million employment internationally and produced over £108 billion in revenue yearly, including £20 billion in exports in 2016 (Duckett, 2018). A PwC study noted that Artificial Intelligence may increase the UK’s GDP by $232 billion by 2030 (Charania and Li, 2020), which would lead to the creation of new jobs and the recruitment of more skilled workers and students into the agriculture industry.

One of the main economic advantages of robots is efficient input management, which can immediately boost farm productivity and profitability through precision planting, efficient harvesting, and reduced use of pesticides, water, and fertilisers (Yahya, 2018). Automation in food handling, packaging, and processing can lower labour costs and food waste (Dai and Cladwell, 2010).

**Environmental Impact**

Robotics and artificial intelligence have the potential to significantly improve the environment. Numerous species of British animals are now in risk due to current farming practises, which also heavily rely on chemicals and large machinery and compact the soil (Duckett, 2018). Robotics and artificial intelligence (AI)-based technologies can assist in resolving these problems by employing accuracy to decrease the use of fertilisers and pesticides, water waste, and soil compaction while increasing agricultural yields (Clarke, 2017).

However, there are drawbacks to deploying robotics in agriculture, including the potential for unsafe pesticide usage without human supervision and higher fertiliser use due to lower application costs (Yue, 2009). Robotics implementation is expensive, and only businesses with substantial financial resources can afford to invest in them. Robots can also improve intensive livestock tending systems by designing appropriate watering and feeding that is tailored to the animals’ needs, efficient drug administration, and efficient slaughtering (Lowenberg-DeBoer et al., 2019). However, smaller and more affordable mobile robots could make a difference and allow smaller farms to access this technology.

**Political Impact**

Since the main objective is to lower labour costs, it has been determined that the employment of robots in food production has the potential to replace human labour (Werkheiser, 2018). It is crucial to remember that while some jobs may disappear, others may be created as a result of the fact that robots need human supervision and upkeep (Carr, 2015). This may change the political dynamic between local governments and agricultural owners in rural areas. Election support for policies promoting agricultural development may become more difficult to get in regions where robots have mostly supplanted human labour.

**Challenges of the adoption and utilisation of artificial intelligence and robotics in agriculture**

**The distribution of automation in the future can be unbalanced:**

In the upcoming years, it is anticipated that the worldwide robot market would expand dramatically. Between 2011 and 2013 (Popa, 2011), 9%, 12%, and 8% annual growth rates are predicted for the US, Asia, and Australia, respectively. Robot internet penetration is predicted to increase to 75% by 2045, from a projected 5% level by 2030. However, there are issues with the uneven distribution of industrialization as well as some rural areas’ restricted access to resources and technology, which could obstruct the use of AI and robotics in agriculture (Eli-Chukwu and Ogwugwam, 2019). As a result, the approval procedure for AI in agriculture may be more difficult and take longer. It is yet unknown if artificial intelligence and robotics will be able to considerably increase food production in these regions by overcoming natural land-based constraints (Popa, 2011).

**Differences between the control studies and the effective application:**

Due to many circumstances such shifting lighting conditions, intricate backgrounds, and capture angles, the photographs produced in the field may be different from those shot under control settings. In addition, additional elements including the soil, insects, and inert materials may cause morphological variation in crops cultivated in the field. In order to increase the generalisation capability of the system, it is crucial to gather a bigger and more diversified collection of tracking data due to the complex physiological characteristics of crops. Although there are few instances, algorithms like Convolution Neural Networks (CNN) and Deep Belief Networks (DBN) may be able to process complicated data in the future (Diego and Riederb, 2018). Processing pertinent data first will help response times. The efficiency and effectiveness with which a system completes tasks determines how valuable it is economically, and this has a big impact on user preference. Customers put accuracy first while cutting back on work (Eli-Chukwu and Ogwugwam, 2019).

**Safety and Confidentiality**

The use of blocker tags for the Internet of Things as well as cryptographic protocols, tag periodicity adjustments, label deletion strategies, and other security countermeasures are all being used to prevent hardware attacks. Location-based services are still susceptible to device capture attacks, where a malicious party can seize total control of the device and its data. Furthermore, hacking is possible during data transmission from the device to the portal (Elijah O. Member, 2018). The farm’s automatic procedures can be interfered with by manipulating the cloud servers where the data is ultimately stored. Data theft, unauthorised login attempts, and attacks that cause a denial of service can all occur in cloud networks. Numerous security policies, including cryptographic techniques, data flow control laws, and personal verification, are being adopted at different levels to address these security problems (Tzounis et al., 2017).

**Agricultural robotics and artificial intelligence difficulties and solutions**

The use of robotics and artificial intelligence in agribusiness is still in its infancy, and its full effects are still unknown. AI and robotics have complex and varied implications on agriculture, varying between established and developing nations, corporations, and agribusinesses. At the moment, financial, technological, societal, and cultural barriers to AI and robotics exist. Any new AI, robots, or related goods must be linked with farmers’ requirements, interests, and abilities in order to overcome adoption barriers. Those working in the food and farming industries must be aware of how customer preferences are changing if artificial intelligence and robotics are to have an impact on agriculture. A group of biologists who are knowledgeable about the possibilities of key technology and AI components is also urgently needed, as is the training of a new generation of physical engineers versed in biological concepts and agricultural practises. Engineering improvements in areas like neurology, plant biotechnology, and soil health will be made possible by future generations of engineers, enabling society to work together in these areas (Donaldson, 2019).

There are rising worries about the use of some smart technologies, and a growing understanding of how some radical technologies have the potential to transform our agricultural society. Robotics technologies can compensate for manpower losses and use inputs more wisely, but they also have the potential to be harmful. It might have detrimental effects on farmers and the global community as a whole (Whitfield et al., 2018). Thus, important factors like anticipation, inclusion, reflexivity, and responsiveness are taken into account while discussing responsible innovation (Bronson, 2018). According to Rose et al. (2018), responsible innovation takes into account those who will be impacted by the use of new agrobots, such as farmers and rural communities, and gives them the opportunity to question the productivity benefits as well as any social, ethical, or environmental concerns while suggesting the necessary changes to innovators and policymakers.

**Conclusion**

This article provides an overview of recent agricultural robotics and artificial intelligence developments. Although the scope is limited to a few inventions and feasible solutions, it sheds light on the advances in this area. The article also explores the potential societal, economic, and environmental implications of the widespread adoption of these technologies. The agriculture industry has utilised rule-based expert systems and artificial neural networks in the past, but deep learning models and computer vision systems are becoming more common. For example, some robots can precisely spray pesticides on target areas, robots that use precision gardening, IoT-based smart irrigation systems, automated crop harvesting robots, and more. As agriculture shifts from a labour-intensive to a high-tech industry, there will be new growth opportunities and the emergence of innovative companies with creative products.

# Challenges in Implementing Agriculture Robots and How to Overcome Them

Feb, 2025 - by
CMI | Category : Smart Technologies

![Challenges in Implementing Agriculture Robots and How to Overcome Them](https://www.coherentmarketinsights.com/blogimg/1739355870.jpg "Challenges in Implementing Agriculture Robots and How to Overcome Them")

The emerging sector of agricultural robotics shows a lot of promise for changing the industry. Still, several farmers find it hard to embrace this leading technology. Other issues are limited availability of resources, need of technical expertise, and great cost. Solving these problems would give us a realistic answer and thus create the groundwork for effective [agriculture robotics](../market-insight/agriculture-robots-market-1183).

### **1.High Initial Costs: A Major Barrier to Adopting Farming Robots**

One big impediment to agricultural robotics usage is their significant initial cost. Including autonomous tractors, drones, and harvesters, these sophisticated technologies might be cost. Such expenditures might be difficult for little-scale farmers or those with very little capital.

**Solution:** By seeking rental or financing options, so, farmers will have the technology more available free of the officially given cost. Moreover government assistance and funding for agricultural innovations help to save money. Sharing equipment with farmers next door also reduces robotic cost.

### **2. Lack of Technical Skills**

Given the need of particular technical knowledge, people have trouble embracing agricultural robots. Particularly in more remote areas, several farmers might be short of vital instruction or support.

**Solution:** For farmers to go to classes given by equipment producers or Agritech company helps to address this issue. Many robotics businesses present online or seminar products for farmers to familiarize themselves with technology. Also helping to ensure a smooth automation transition would be a partnership with Agritech companies throughout the learning process.

### **3. Infrastructure Limitations**

In some rural areas, current infrastructure would not work well for smooth running of farm robots. This addresses issues of bad ground conditions for robotics, low charging station availability, and slow internet access.

**Solution:** To support the data transfer needed for robotic applications, farmers could need better internet access through regional networks or satellite services. Moreover, some companies are developing devices intended to adapt to several surroundings, ensuring these systems will function in several land types and situations.

### **4. Uncertainty About Return on Investment (ROI)**

Many ranchers are reluctant to get robotics for fear of little return on investment. Sometimes one may be slow to embrace new technology since it is not clear how automation—that is, labor savings, increased efficiency, and high output—can assist.

**Solution:** One solution could be for farmers to start small -scale robotic technologies including planting or weeding robots to observe their impact and return on investment. By gradually accepting robotics, farmers can learn and adapt without disturbing their activities.

### **5. Regulatory and Safety Concerns**

Slowing the acceptance of agricultural robotics are regulatory issues in such labor rules and security levels. The applications of autonomous systems highlight concerns about their impact on the labour force and their adherence to present rules.

**Solution:** One way out is for farmers to stay up on local and federal robotics and automation regulations. Engagement with exactly businesses and politicians will help to remain ahead of legal changes and ensure adherence to standards. Over time too, robots are reluctantly permitted to tackle the labor displacement fears and this helps to run and maintain mechanical systems.

### **6. Resistance to Change**

At last, fear of change or ignorance keeps lots of farmers from using modern technologies. Switching to robotics would seem rather difficult since conventional agricultural methods have worked for hundreds of years.

**Solution:** By using robots that buttress their current methods rather than outright replacing them, farmers first can enhance technology. Robotic systems for checking soil quality, for example, could fit with current farming methods with little or no changes needed. Furthermore, offering successful cases of different farmers who have efficiently adopted robotics will help to reduce stress and foster acceptance.

### **Conclusion: Overcoming Obstacles in Farm Automation**

Although challenges are vital for adopting agricultural robotics, they are not insurmountable. Handling challenges such high cost, skill gaps, infrastructure restrictions, and legal issues allow farmers to totally adopt the advantages of agricultural robotics. Proper approaches can help the agriculture sector to advance toward more automation, therefore raising both stability and output.

Starting small, constantly learning, and leveraging current resources and collaboration can help farmers quick to recognize robotics make the change simpler. Major recommendations for implementing agricultural robotics entail phasing the technology, seeking financial support, and committing to continuous learning and training. Farmers may negotiate the obstacles of automation and welcome a future in which technology boosts the activity of agriculture by following these steps.

Abstract
--------

In a world that requires sustainable agricultural practices, quantitative plant-by-plant and field status monitoring can be advantageous to all farmers by optimizing their [farm management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farming-management "Learn more about farm management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Autonomous robots, sensing technologies, and automated data analysis will play a key role. In the present article, we discuss three critical aspects of the incorporation of field robots in [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): 1) the main design specifications and economic aspects for Commercial Agricultural Robots (CARs) in terms of [autonomous navigation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/autonomous-navigation "Learn more about autonomous navigation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (perception, localization, and kinematics); 2) the business models of agricultural robotics companies, which are compared to successful business models of robotic companies on the medical field; 3) the possibilities that CARs could bring, including the generation of [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") about local [food production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/food-production "Learn more about food production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and its impact on the local environment. Our analysis highlights the reasons that explain the low adoption of CARs in the current agricultural market. Finally, we reflect on the current distorted costs that consumers pay for food while the [ecological footprints](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/ecological-footprint "Learn more about ecological footprints from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of food production and food delivery are neglected, and we encourage the discussion to promote sustainable food policies that support the establishment of robotic agricultural companies.

1. Introduction
---------------

The [Food and Agriculture Organization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/food-and-agriculture-organization "Learn more about Food and Agriculture Organization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FAO) of the United Nations expressed in 2017 that there has been undeniable progress in reducing undernourishment rates and in improving nutrition and health levels, but also that in 2050 there will be a need of 50% more food compared to 2013 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0001). Regrettably, there is also evidence that scaled-up current [food production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/food-production "Learn more about food production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leads to varied and significant harmful environmental consequences [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0003), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0004), such as the apparition of superweeds, the damage of [farmland biodiversity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farmland-biodiversity "Learn more about farmland biodiversity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the uncontrolled spread of pesticides by rain clouds.

This paper considers the EU region as an example of these needs and specific problems to solve. In 2013, the percentage of European farm holders of very small-sized farms, small-sized farms, and medium-sized farms was 40%, 29%, and 14%, respectively [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0005). In such farms, known as Small and Medium-sized Farms (SMFs), two field management practices can be identified: 1) Precision Agriculture (PA), meaning to perform relevant crop measurements to collect on-site crop data to maximize the yield of food production while minimizing the use of fuel, herbicide, insecticide and nutrients; and 2) an agro-ecosystem way, meaning to consider and observe the strong ecological interactions that make plants grow (e.g., [agroecology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/agroecology "Learn more about agroecology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and agroforestry). The agro-ecosystem management underlies in an understanding of the local ecosystem services, acquired iteratively and holistically by farmers that live more in contact with nature. These farmers scarify fields to build resilience, employing old seeds varieties, indirect pest management techniques, and ancient weeding techniques due to their holistic understanding of biotic interactions in the field [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0006).

To the authors’ view, the shortcomings of both approaches are the lack of comprehensibility. In our humble understanding of the topic, it is infeasible to measure everything relevant to crops and soils for a farmland with sufficient detail/resolution for PA [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0001). Even if possible in an affordable way, it will be difficult for a person to make good use of this [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). On the agro-ecosystem approach [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0002), the infeasibility is to demand that farmers achieve a detailed understanding of farm ecosystems, which can be translated into field management actions that maximize both yields and [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ecosystem preservation).

Therefore, as a solution to these weaknesses in field management, we study the feasibility of combining the best of these approaches using robotic solutions. The introduction of [robotic systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/robotic-system "Learn more about robotic systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the [crop fields](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/field-crops "Learn more about crop fields from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enables highly repeatable systematic actions, such as selective [mechanical weeding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mechanical-weed-control "Learn more about mechanical weeding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (which reduces the proliferation of superweeds) and health monitoring of crop and soil. In this paper, we term these robots as Commercial Agricultural Robots (CARs). Also, we use the term robotic companies to refer to companies that develop the technology, manufacture the robot or work with a 3rd party manufacturer.

However, the adoption of CARs by owners of SMFs implies facing a potentially high cost of technology integration.It is important to mention that common trends in the last decades have been the increase of the farms size, increase of automation levels and decrease of the amount of farmers and workers [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0007). In such a scenario, most of the CARs are in use at the more resourceful farms, usually big and very big farms. Nevertheless, small, sophisticated and economically accessible robots could change this trend and make CARs available to smaller farms [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0008),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0009)]; which is also important as farmers can use their immediately free time in new ventures (e.g., adding value to some or all of their production by transforming it into a local end-user product) or in expanding existing activities.In any case, the technology integration cost becomes critical [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0010) both for the profitability of the companies and for the farmers.

Other challenges for adopting CARs are maintenance and cost in terms of hardware and software, and the time span to assess the economic and environmental benefits of the robot and its performance. These activities are additional and very different from the traditional activities performed by most farmers; therefore, they surely may discourage the incorporation and use of CARs.

In terms of the adoption and sustainability studies to assess smart agricultural, an economic study [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0011) analyzed three different robotic weeding applications for the particular case of high-value crops and concluded that the cost of [robotic applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/robotics-application "Learn more about robotic applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was lower than the cost of conventional systems. Remarkably, the authors pointed out that sensors for precision localization and the small labor capacity of the vehicles were the main cost contributors, which may hinder the use of robots in low value crops. A recent and broader economic study [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0012) explained that estimating the use cost of CARs in [arable land](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arable-land "Learn more about arable land from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), similarly to conventional [agricultural machinery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-machinery "Learn more about agricultural machinery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), is by far inexact. The reason is the lack of the definition of critical operational parameters (inexistence of normative). Thus, they proposed a preliminary methodology to conduct an agricultural cost-benefit analysis when robotic systems are employed in [arable farming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/arable-farming "Learn more about arable farming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

The remaining of the paper has been structured as follows. Section 2 introduces techniques used by CAR to autonomously navigate across the farm. Section 3 develops a budget estimation for robots used in farms. Section 4 describes the business models adopted by agricultural robotics companies. Section 5 gives an analysis of successful business models of robotics companies. Section 6 outlines scenarios that may lead to sustainable farm production using ARs (Agricultural Robots). Finally, Section 7 concludes on the advantages of different ARs business models and the [autonomous navigation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/autonomous-navigation "Learn more about autonomous navigation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strategies compatible with them.

2. Background: autonomous navigation for commercial agricultural robots
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

CARs need to perceive the environment, locate themselves and navigate through a [crop field](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/field-crops "Learn more about crop field from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to perform specific tasks. Sensors and navigation strategies are two of the most crucial aspects for developing [unmanned ground vehicles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/unmanned-ground-vehicles "Learn more about unmanned ground vehicles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with autonomous capabilities. The needs in terms of sensors and cost vary greatly according to the type of environment (unstructured vs. structured environments) in which the robot will operate. Thus, the choice of sensors will have a significant impact on the unit's final cost and on the robot's navigation abilities.

In structured environments, a successful case is that of [Automated Guided Vehicles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/automated-guided-vehicle "Learn more about Automated Guided Vehicles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AGVs) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0013). For example, in automobile factories, autonomous or semi-autonomous trolleys move goods inside warehouses. These trolleys are tracked by AGV technology to operate on supportive infrastructure for self-localization in a predefined map. Supportive infrastructure implies the integration of simple magnetic tape tracks on the floor, wires under the floor emitting a magnetic signature, or landmarks across the factory to be identified by lasers. These navigation strategies, in structured environments, facilitate the navigation autonomy and safety of the vehicle with a smaller number of sensors. In [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), this kind of technology can be used for some tasks in greenhouses, but it is not directly applicable to outdoor and unstructured environments.

On the other hand, CARs can operate in outdoor and in unstructured environments. They typically use a [Global Navigation Satellite System](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/gnss "Learn more about Global Navigation Satellite System from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (GNSS) for localization in the field. Examples of such systems are [GPS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-positioning-system "Learn more about GPS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from the United States, [GLONASS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/glonass "Learn more about GLONASS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from Russia, [BDS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/bds "Learn more about BDS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from China, and Galileo from the European Union. However, to enable long-term autonomy, it is desirable to have systems that do not rely exclusively on GNSS because of the signal shortage in parts of the fields and under the canopy. Non-GNSS-based localization techniques such as SLAM (Simultaneous Location And Mapping) in its different varieties [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0015), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0016), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0017), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0018) work well in various applications. Still, SLAM techniques are not well suited for agricultural environments where plants are located with regular, repetitive spacing and are intentionally grown to be as uniform as possible [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0019). Therefore, robust localization in the field demands the simultaneous use of different techniques. The selection of the navigation strategy to be used in an unstructured environment is a crucial challenge in CARs development.

As required capabilities of self-localization and navigation may vary drastically depending on the field and application, the implemented design of the robot can impact the number and the type of sensor to endow the CARs. In fact, as the type and number of sensors increase, CARs can perform more complex tasks. For these reasons, the challenges of CARs arise from the difficulty of creating high-performance robots and specific navigation strategies while ensuring safety and legal requirements for autonomous operation [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0020).

The following section describes robot navigation strategies, kinematics, power train, and sensors used for self-localization and navigation in robots developed in research labs during the last few years to provide a technical baseline of agricultural robots. In addition, the cost of hardware components is given so that the total cost of CARs navigation hardware can be later estimated.

### 2.1. Features of agricultural robots for research

We considered six agricultural robots reported in the literature: [Strathclyde](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/strathclyde "Learn more about Strathclyde from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0021), two Husky-based (GRAPE [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0022) and agRob [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0023)), [LadyBird](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/coccinellidae "Learn more about LadyBird from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0024), Robotanist [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0025), and BoniRob [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0026). They were chosen to give a wide and diverse technological baseline (showed in [Scheme 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#fig0001)) for a future comparison to CARs.

As ROS (Robot Operating System) is being used for robot navigation and control on most robots, a brief introduction of this platform follows. Nowadays, general-purpose software for robot development is a reality after a decade of evolution. The Open Source Robotics Foundation (OSRF) maintains two combined open-source products called ROS and Gazebo. ROS is a robotics middleware composed of a large set of software libraries, conventions for data exchange between different processes, and data visualization tools. And Gazebo is a simulation tool to evaluate ROS-based robots in a 3D virtual environment.

The adoption of ROS in both research and industry settings is very significant. We mention that Tier-1 suppliers are successfully using it for [rapid prototyping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/rapid-prototyping "Learn more about rapid prototyping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). While the manufacturing industry aims to integrate advanced versions of ROS (ROS-Industrial) in the final automated product [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0027). Similar adoption was publicly initiated in the US military industry.

The navigation strategy of the six agricultural robots previously mentioned, which operate under ROS, are described below:
*   1.Strathclyde: It is a small rover prototype for a future agricultural robot developed by the University of Strathclyde [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0021). The researchers assessed the [localization error](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/localization-error "Learn more about localization error from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the rover on navigation targets for different combinations of sensors. Its [autonomous navigation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/autonomous-navigation "Learn more about autonomous navigation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") capability was based on a generic motion model implemented on standard ROS libraries [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277237552200034X#bib0028) and off-the-shelf hardware, such as a stereo camera, ultrasound range sensors, a scanning Laser Rangefinder (LRF), and an Inertial [Measurement Unit](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/units-of-measurement "Learn more about Measurement Unit from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IMU). This robot only uses standard ROS libraries to perform Visual Odometry (VO), SLAM, and global and local [path planning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/trajectory-planning "Learn more about path planning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), among other tasks. A GNSS receiver was employed as a first approximation of the rover location. Finally, they compared experiments conducted in the lab against outdoor ones, explaining the difficulties encountered regarding visual perception, particularly for the visual feature extraction in outdoor lighting. 
*   2.GRAPE (Husky-based): It is a robot built with off-the-shelf hardware (i.e., including a Husky robot) elaborated for vineyard applications. Fast Facts
----------

Precision agriculture uses technologies such as GPS or automation to make farms more efficient. For example, farmers can use auto-steering equipment to precisely plant a field, and activity monitors can help dairy farmers collect data about their cows' health.

In this _Technology Assessment_, we review the benefits and challenges of precision agriculture technologies. They can make farms more profitable and have environmental benefits—like reducing fertilizer runoff in waterways. But they can also be complex and have high up-front costs, making it hard for some farmers to access them. We examine policy options to help address these challenges.

Highlights

What GAO Found
--------------

Precision agriculture technologies can improve resource management through the precise application of inputs, such as water, fertilizer, and feed, leading to more efficient agricultural production. Precision agriculture can be implemented through a suite of technologies that can be used in isolation or in conjunction with other technologies. Examples of emerging precision agriculture technologies are in the table below.

**Examples of emerging precision agriculture technologies**

| **Technology** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| Remote sensing platforms | Drones and ground robots can provide new ways to provide measurements on crop conditions. |
| In-ground sensors | Provide farmers near-real-time information on soil and plant properties such as temperature, moisture, and nutrients. |
| Targeted spray systems | Use machine learning to precisely spray in a specific spot. |
| Automated mechanical weeders | Use machine learning to start and stop weeding blades to avoid damaging the growing crops. |

Source: GAO summary of literature, interviews, and agency documentation. | GAO-24-105962

While precision agriculture technologies, such as variable rate fertilizer applications and yield monitoring, have been available since the 1990s, only 27 percent of U.S. farms or ranches used precision agriculture practices to manage crops or livestock, based on 2023 U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) reporting.

**Use of precision agriculture practices by U.S. farms, June 2022–June 2023**

![Image 5](https://www.gao.gov/assets/870/865369.png)

Federal agencies support precision agriculture adoption, research and development, education, and training. USDA supports precision agriculture technology adoption with financial assistance and loan programs, such as through payments for implementing practices that provide a conservation benefit. USDA and the National Science Foundation (NSF) have provided almost $200 million for precision agriculture research and development funding in fiscal years 2017—2021. This funding includes partnerships between the two agencies to support artificial intelligence (AI) research institutes.

Benefits to using precision agriculture technologies include:

*   **Increased profits.** Farmers can increase yields and thus profits with the same amount of inputs or achieve an equivalent yield with fewer inputs.
*   **Reduced application of crop inputs.** Technologies can reduce the application of crop inputs such as fertilizer, herbicide, fuel, and water. They can also address water scarcity by promoting the efficient use of water in agriculture.
*   **Environmental benefits.** Technologies can prevent excessive use of chemicals and nutrients in a field, potentially reducing runoff into soil and waterways.

Challenges limiting the broader adoption and use of precision agriculture include:

*   **High up-front acquisition costs.** Acquisition costs for the latest technologies can be prohibitive for farmers with limited resources or access to capital.
*   **Farm data sharing and ownership issues.** Concerns regarding farm data sharing and ownership can pose obstacles to the widespread use of AI in agriculture.
*   **Lack of standards.** An absence of uniform standards can hamper interoperability between different precision agriculture technologies.

GAO examined three policy goals and associated options that could help address adoption challenges or enhance the benefits of precision agriculture technologies. These policy options identify possible actions by policymakers, which include Congress, federal agencies, state and local governments, academic and research institutions, and industry. In addition, for each policy goal, policymakers may choose no additional policy interventions, maintaining the status quo by continuing existing activities.

**Policy Goals and Options That Could Address Challenges or Help Enhance Benefits of Adoption and Use of Precision Agriculture Technologies**

| **Policy goals and options** | **Implementation approaches** | **Opportunities and considerations** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Encourage greater adoption and use(report p. 43)** |
| Provide additional incentives or other financial support | * Consider modifying eligibility criteria for existing governmental financial assistance programs. * Consider expanding levels of financial assistance through new or existing programs. | * Consider what types of programs to provide to farmers, such as loan guarantees or grants. * Overall program cost, the extent to which equipment acquisition and operating costs are covered, and the potential trade-off with other agricultural programs could be factors. * Programs could be devised to realize goals, such as environmental protection and food insecurity. |
| Better understand and quantify benefits and costs | * Support development of analytical tools and models to quantify benefits and costs. | * Analytical tools could be configured for farm geography, soil type, and other factors. * Farmer confidence could increase if estimates of benefits are data-driven and based on real-world experiences. |
| Promotion and outreach to farmers | * Consider expanding Extension services to enable development of more expertise and technical support to farmers. * Increase on-field demonstrations. | * The extent to which federal agencies could provide more training, such as through USDA service centers. * Technology companies and dealerships could assume more of a role in educating or helping farmers. |
| **Encourage further innovation(report p. 48)** |
| Conduct research and development to improve on-farm data gathering and analysis | * Support research for in-ground sensors for measuring soil conditions. * Examine ways that remote imagery, such as from drones, can be more effectively used to gather data. | * In-ground sensors can provide more detailed information on soil conditions, which can enable farmers to further optimize the application of inputs to increase yields and minimize costs. * New remote imagery sensors from drones or ground robots promise to provide greater resolution, more frequent data, and quicker data delivery than traditional satellite sources. |
| Promote the development and use of standards | * Promote data standards that could improve equipment interoperability. * Financial assistance programs could specify that precision agriculture equipment comply with certain standards. | * Standards can improve interoperability and compatibility among different devices and platforms. * A lack of standards could result in challenges assessing the quality of the data derived from farm measurements. * Consider the level of federal involvement in standards development and use. |
| **Manage greater amounts of data(report p. 52)** |
| Enhance data analysis | * Support the development of software to help farmers better manage their farms. * Examine how AI and machine learning could help facilitate analysis and interpretation of data. | * Data analytics and high-performance computing approaches promise to generate valuable information for farmers but are dependent on the availability of large amounts of data. * Farmers may have tools, such as yield monitors, to help identify outcomes of decisions, but few analytical tools and software are available to enhance analysis and translate farm data into actionable decisions. |
| Encourage data sharing | * Develop a governance framework to manage and store agricultural data and its access. * Establish easy-to-understand data license agreements and codes of conduct to enable better flow of data. | * Farmers often do not trust the ways farm data are being collected and managed; thus, terms and conditions regarding data use should be simple, transparent, and accountable. * Farmer concerns about data sharing include a potential loss of competitive advantage, data security, and additional regulatory scrutiny may increase as AI is increasingly used for data analysis. |

Source: GAO. | GAO-24-105962

Why GAO Did This Study
----------------------

Precision agriculture involves collecting, analyzing, and taking actions based on data. It can help the agricultural sector meet increasing demand for food products, while also helping farmers improve efficiencies such as through reduced input costs.

The Advancing IoT for Precision Agriculture Act of 2021, contained in what is commonly referred to as the CHIPS and Science Act of 2022, included provisions for GAO to conduct a technology assessment and review federal programs.

This report examines (1) emerging precision agriculture technologies and precision agriculture technology adoption; (2) federal programs providing support for precision agriculture; (3) benefits and challenges of adopting and using precision agriculture technologies; and (4) policy options that could address challenges or help enhance benefits of adopting and using precision agriculture technologies.

To conduct this technology assessment and review, GAO reviewed scientific literature and other key reports; interviewed officials and representatives from government, industry, academia, and associations; conducted two site visits to observe technology operations and obtain stakeholder perspectives; and convened a 3-day virtual expert meeting in collaboration with the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. GAO is identifying policy options in this report.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The adoption of agricultural robotics involves significant upfront investment in technology, making it financially challenging, especially for small-scale farmers with limited capital resources.
Concerns over who owns and controls the data generated by agricultural robots can hinder adoption, as farmers fear loss of privacy and potential misuse of their information.

The absence of uniform standards across different robotic systems complicates integration and interoperability, leading to inefficiencies and increased costs for farmers implementing new technologies.
Farmers often exhibit reluctance to adopt robotics due to comfort with traditional methods, fear of job displacement, and uncertainty about the effectiveness of new technologies.
